name: Past Tense (passato prossimo)
summary: The everyday past tense, built with avere or essere plus a past participle.
explanation:
The passato prossimo is the main past tense for completed actions. It has two parts: a helper verb (avere or essere) in the present, plus the past participle of the main verb.

Regular participles end in -ato (-are verbs), -uto (-ere verbs), and -ito (-ire verbs): parlato, venduto, finito. So ho parlato means 'I spoke' or 'I have spoken'.

Most verbs use avere. But verbs of movement and change (andare, venire, essere, nascere) use essere, and then the participle agrees with the subject: lei è andata, loro sono andati.
commonMistakes: Using avere with movement verbs. Andare, venire, and arrivare take essere.
Forgetting participle agreement with essere. A woman says sono andata, not andato.
Leaving the helper verb out. The participle alone is not a full past tense.
Trying to make the participle agree when the helper is avere (in basic use it stays fixed: ho mangiato).
Confusing irregular participles; fare gives fatto, vedere gives visto, dire gives detto.
tip: Memorise the short list of essere verbs (motion and change). Everything else defaults to avere.
